questions.question_text: Which best describes the graph of $y=12-5x$?

options.why: Read the coefficient of $x$ as $+5$, missing the minus sign in $-5x$.
Mistook the constant $12$ for the gradient; the coefficient of $x$ is $-5$, so the line slopes.
Matched the numbers but the coefficient is $-5$; parallel lines need the same coefficient, not the opposite sign.
Matched the constant term $-12$; parallel lines need the same coefficient of $x$, not the same $y$-intercept.
Swapped the coefficient and constant; the coefficient of $x$ must stay $5$.

questions.solution_text:
$y=5x-12$ has coefficient $5$
$y=5x+3$ has coefficient $5$
Same coefficient, so parallel

questions.question_text: Which line has the same $y$-intercept as $y=3x-4$?

options.why: Kept the coefficient $3$ but changed the constant; the $y$-intercept comes from the constant term $-4$.
Used $-4$ as the coefficient; the $y$-intercept is the constant term, not the coefficient of $x$.
Swapped $3$ and $-4$; the $y$-intercept is the constant term, which must be $-4$.

questions.solution_text:
$y=3x-4$ has constant term $-4$
$y=-2x-4$ has constant term $-4$
Same constant term, so same $y$-intercept

questions.question_text: Which of these lines is the steepest: $y=2x+7$, $y=x-3$ or $y=4x$?

options.why: Picked the line with the largest constant term; steepness comes from the coefficient of $x$, and $4>2$.
Chose the simplest coefficient; a coefficient of $1$ gives the least steep line here.

questions.solution_text:
$y=2x+7$ has coefficient $2$
$y=x-3$ has coefficient $1$
$y=4x$ has coefficient $4$
$4$ is the largest, so $y=4x$ is the steepest

questions.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[width=6cm,height=6cm,axis lines=middle,axis line style={-{Stealth}},xmin=-3,xmax=3,ymin=-4,ymax=5,xtick={-3,-2,...,3},ytick={-4,-2,...,4},minor y tick num=1,grid=both,clip=false,scaled ticks=false,ticklabel style={/pgf/number format/fixed},xlabel={$x$},ylabel={$y$}]\addplot[thick,blue!70!black,<->,domain=-2.4:1.8,samples=2] {2*x+1} node[pos=0.9,right,text=blue!70!black] {$A$};\addplot[thick,red!75!black,dashed,<->,domain=-0.9:3,samples=2] {2*x-2} node[pos=0.9,right,text=red!75!black] {$B$};\addplot[thick,green!50!black,dotted,<->,domain=-2.8:3,samples=2] {-x+1} node[pos=0.9,below,text=green!50!black] {$C$};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
The graph shows $A:y=2x+1$, $B:y=2x-2$ and $C:y=-x+1$. Which two lines are parallel?

options.why: $A$ and $C$ cross the $y$-axis at the same point but have different coefficients of $x$, so they are not parallel.
These have different coefficients ($2$ and $-1$); only $A$ and $B$ share the coefficient of $x$.
Parallel lines must share one coefficient of $x$; $C$ has coefficient $-1$, unlike $A$ and $B$.

questions.solution_text:
$A:y=2x+1$ has coefficient $2$
$B:y=2x-2$ has coefficient $2$
$C:y=-x+1$ has coefficient $-1$
Same coefficient $2$, so $A$ and $B$ are parallel

questions.question_text: Which of these lines is the steepest: $y=-4x+1$, $y=3x-2$, $y=2x$ or $y=-x+5$?

options.why: Took the largest positive coefficient; steepness uses the magnitude, and $|-4|>3$.
Picked the line with the largest constant term; the constant is the $y$-intercept, not the steepness.
Assumed a positive gradient must be steeper than a negative one; magnitude $4$ beats $2$.

questions.solution_text:
magnitudes of the coefficients: $|-4|=4$, $|3|=3$, $|2|=2$, $|-1|=1$
$4$ is the largest, so $y=-4x+1$ is the steepest
